posts.text:
This war has big Napoleon III vibes to me.

slowboring.com/p/a-rawlsian-…

The Iranians think they can beat us by raising the global price of energy, but Trump is way too smart for that and he’s fighting back by lifting sanctions on Iranian oil exports to ease the supply crunch.
Timing on this call was not ideal.

Whatever the affordability crisis is, it is compatible with both incomes and consumption (yes, both adjusted for inflation) being higher than ever.  

slowboring.com/p/affordabili…

Something I would like to read about in detail is why is there such a large gap between the commission of the crime and the imposition of consequences? 

Everyone agrees that swiftly is more impactful than severity per se, but we are very much not delivering that.


U.S. Attorney DC (@USAO_DC)

Today, Reginald Steele, Jr., 26, was sentenced for killing 13-year-old Malachi Lukes, on March 1, 2020, and for a separate shooting in the Petworth neighborhood on Feb. 22, 2020.

Steele and three co-defe
Do you generally think movies are too long these days?

Now a question that I do have for proponents of supply constriction as a climate approach is this:

Given that we can all see Trump's war is economically harmful, can't we see that  cutting off domestic supply would be even more harmful than cutting off Gulf supply?

But it is not even remotely true that I support Donald Trump's Iran policy, this is a topic I have written about extensively and where my views are not even slightly ambiguous. 

slowboring.com/p/nobody-plan…

A true fact about me is that I do not believe the United States should try to address climate change by constraining domestic fossil fuel production. 

slowboring.com/p/the-case-ag…

One of the toxic things about Jeff Hauser is that even though he and I have real policy disagreements, he absolutely refuses to articulate and debate them insisting on arguing *exclusively* through misrepresentation, innuendo, and slander.
It's sort of funny that we're going to have AGI before we have a printer that isn't constantly breaking.

I caution against thinking too hard about this. 

slowboring.com/p/affordabili…

There's been this huge influx of "affordability" jargon, but:

1. We had a bunch of inflation 
2. Trump promised the price level would fall
3. The rate of increase never even fell back to the target level
4. It's now re-accelerating


Mike Dorning (@MikeDorning)

Investors are now betting overall US inflation over the next 12 months will surge above 5%. Implied inflation rate from 1 year breakeven, per Bloomberg @business @TheTerminal





— https://nitter.net/MikeDorning/status/2035017075442233

By the same token, though, friendly Caribbean islands who rely almost entirely on LNG and fuel foil for electricity and cannot function without maritime shipping of ~everything are getting wrecked as collateral damage.

jamaicaobserver.com/2026/03/…

Iran crisis almost certainly makes the Cuba blockade more effective because it’s very easy to find willing customers elsewhere, minimizing the incentive to test whether Trump is bluffing about this.

usnews.com/news/top-news/art…

They are rationing cooking fuel in India whether Brent is at $100 or $115

theguardian.com/world/2026/m…

One thing to keep in mind is that the demand-destruction is happening in the market for specific products — jet fuel, naphtha, diesel — so to an extent all this WTI jawboning is just impacting crack spreads anyway.


Matthew Zeitlin (@MattZeitlin)

excited to see whatever headline is going to try to keep WTI under $100 over the weekend



— https://nitter.net/MattZeitlin/status/2034994469351624932#m

I think JON OSSOFF might have the best chance of being a candidate who gets real credit from progressives for breaking with Biden on arms sales to Israel without having a particularly left-wing record. 

The opposite of the Newsom/Harris profile. 

www.slowboring.com/p/the-obama-...
My intuition aligns with yours, but the research indicates that we are wrong which is why I thought it was interesting to write about.
No I'm not, ask the authors if you want.
Say the Supreme Court got it right and the government should enforce that.

Notable to the discourse, moderating on trans issues is not particularly high-leverage. 

The best things to move right on are (in order) racial preferences, teachers unions, sexual orientation curriculum in schools, letting in asylum-seekers, punishing non-violent criminals, and fossil fuels.

So the upshot here is that rather than moderating at random, Democrats should stick with conventional progressive defense of Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid while also adopting views that are to the right of the entire existing caucus on a few key issues.

What is the single most effective issue to move to the center on? 

Affirmative action in college admissions — an issue that no Democrat in recent cycles has been moderate on. 

Obviously moderation doesn't work if you don't do it!

And to be clear "moderate" in this research does not mean "like Kamala Harris or Gavin Newsom as opposed to Bernie" — it means "be more right-wing than mainstream establishment Democrats." 

But it does also help explain why the observed benefits of moderation in recent cycles are small.

Sorry to give a less measured take, this research indicates that just adopting more moderate views at random — though definitely not optimal — will on average help you win elections. 

If you have no survey research or data or anything, just go with your gut and be more moderate.

As Morris has documented extensively, the electoral benefits of moderation, though real, are also not enormous. 

The paper suggests that moderating SELECTIVELY — on affirmative action, merit pay for teachers, punishing non-violent crime, and using fossil fuels — would be much more potent.

As @gelliottmorris.com says, the research offers more nuanced advice than "just be more moderate." 

It says that moderation is beneficial on average, but that the impact of moderation varies issue-to-issue and that on a minority of issues moderation actually backfires.
I promise that I discussed this paper with the authors, that's why I had exclusive early access to write a New York Times op-ed about it.
I like that he's for school prayer AND for sex stores

The YIMBY movement has come to encompass a wide range of themes and ideas — single stair, missing middle, manufactured homes — and achieved national scope but the core question of growth (or lack thereof) in a handful of very high cost areas remains crucial. 

www.slowboring.com/p/why-silico...
In my defense, the text was wrapped up days ago
If I had a barrel of West Texas Intermediate crude oil laying around somewhere, I would not sell it for $100 given the current circumstances.
I think Caraballo is just restating the problem here not offering an alternative solution.

Today in the NYT, I discuss some fresh research on which moderations move the most voters.

www.nytimes.com/2026/03/16/o...
This is an amazing platform

If per person spending on the elderly goes up and the elderly share of the population also goes up, everything else gets squeezed and everyone is mad.

But it’s what the voters say they want.

www.slowboring.com/p/the-demoni...
In defense of having a dumb thing to care about (by Halina Bennet). It’s your civic responsibility to have a March Madness bracket.
The racial justice case for zoning reform  (by Matthew Yglesias). Plus price discrimination, why men stopped dancing, and the case for Michael B. Jordan
Thursday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
The Obama of 2028? (by Matthew Yglesias). Democrats need a moderate that leftists like — Israel could provide an Iraq-like opening
Washington goes YIMBY? (by Halina Bennet). Everyone’s trying to squeeze in more housing. Well, not everyone.
Worry less, do more (by Matthew Yglesias). Paralyzing anguish isn’t how you help people or solve problems.
Tuesday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Why Silicon Valley hasn’t done more for most Americans (by Matthew Yglesias)
Quinceañeras and Republican tumult (by Halina Bennet)
Upscale liberals are developing class consciousness (by Matthew Yglesias)
Sunday Thread + Mailbag (by Halina Bennet)
Saturday discussion post (by Halina Bennet)
Could the Second Mexican Empire have endured? (by Matthew Yglesias). Plus the case for proportional representation, Obama’s foreign policy, and an S-Bahn for Greater Boston
I don’t buy your “dating recession” (by Halina Bennet). The collapse of romantic infrastructure is an engineering failure, not a Gen Z character flaw.
The demonic policy strangling the British economy  (by Matthew Yglesias)
